title: Hearing - Sales and/or Use Tax (State,Local,MTA) - 202601017H
text:
SOAH DOCKET NO. 304-25-26686

CPA HEARING NO. 120,532

 


RE:      **************


TAXPAYER NO:  **************

AUDIT OFFICE:   **************

AUDIT PERIOD:   November 1, 2014 THROUGH March 31, 2020


Sales And Use Tax/RDT

BEFORE THE COMPTROLLER OF PUBLIC ACCOUNTS

OF THE STATE OF TEXAS


KELLY HANCOCK
Acting Texas Comptroller of Public Accounts


MOLLY ANNE BUCHANAN

Representing Respondent

 

**************

Representing Petitioner


 



COMPTROLLER’S DECISION
 

This decision is considered final on February 2, 2026, unless a motion for rehearing is timely filed; this date of finality is calculated based on the Administrative Procedure Act (APA).[1]  The failure to timely file a motion for rehearing may result in adverse legal consequences.


Administrative Law Judge (ALJ) Kimberly LaFlair of the State Office of Administrative Hearings (SOAH) issued a Proposal for Decision (PFD) that includes Findings of Fact and Conclusions of Law.  SOAH served the PFD on each party and each party was given an opportunity to file exceptions and replies with SOAH in accordance with SOAH’s rules of procedure.  The ALJ recommended that the Comptroller adopt the PFD as written.


After review and consideration, IT IS ORDERED that the PFD is adopted as written. 


The result from this Decision is Attachment A.  The ALJ’s recommendation letter is Attachment B.  The PFD as written is Attachment C.  Attachments A, B, and C are incorporated by reference.


Attachment A reflects a liability.[2]


The total sum of the tax, penalty, and interest is due and payable 20 days after a comptroller’s decision becomes final.[3]  If such sum is not timely paid, an additional penalty of 10 percent of the taxes due will accrue.


SIGNED on this 6th day of January 2026
 

KELLY HANCOCK
Acting Comptroller of Public Accounts


By: Lisa Craven

Deputy Comptroller


Attachment A, Texas Notification of Hearing Results
Attachment B, ALJ’s recommendation letter
Attachment C, Proposal for Decision as written

 

 

 


ATTACHMENT C

 

 

SOAH Docket No. 304-25-26686                                                             

Before the State Office of Administrative Hearings

TCPA Hearing No. 120,532

 

 

Proposal for Decision

 

The Tax Division (Staff) of the Texas Comptroller of Public Accounts (Comptroller) audited ************** (Petitioner) for compliance with sales and use tax laws and made an assessment that included tax, 10% late-filing penalty, 50% fraud penalty, and accrued interest. Petitioner requested redetermination, provided additional documentation, and the audit was amended. Petitioner contends that the amended audit contains multiples errors, and he requests waivers of all penalties and interest. Staff contends Petitioner failed to meet his burden to establish audit error. In this Proposal for Decision, the Administrative Law Judge (ALJ) recommends that the amended assessment be affirmed, except as agreed to by Staff.

 

I. Notice, Jurisdiction, and Procedural History

 

Staff referred the contested case to the State Office of Administrative Hearings (SOAH) and, on August 26, 2025, issued Petitioner a Notice of Hearing by Written Submission. On September 27, 2025, ALJ Kimberly LaFlair issued Order Setting Written Submission Hearing. Petitioner represented himself, and Molly Anne Buchanan represented Staff. The contested case record closed on November 17, 2025.

 

There are no issues of notice or jurisdiction; therefore, those matters are set out in the Findings of Fact and Conclusions of Law without further discussion.

 

II. REASONS FOR DECISION

 

A. Evidence

 

Petitioner did not submit an exhibit list, evidentiary exhibits, or argument for the written submission hearing. Staff submitted the pleadings exchanged between the parties prior to referring the matter to SOAH and offered the following exhibits:

 


	
	Hearing Request Receipt;
	
	
	Hearing Grant Letter;
	
	
	Intro Letter;
	
	
	Texas Notification of Audit Results;
	
	
	Penalty and Interest Waiver Worksheet;
	
	
	Audit Report;
	
	
	Audit Plan;
	
	
	Amended Audit Report; and
	
	
	Amended Audit Plan.
	


 

Staff’s exhibits were admitted without objection.

 

B. Agreed Adjustments

 

Staff agreed to waive interest for the period from March 1, 2025, through May 31, 2025, due to delay in issuing the Position Letter.

 

C. Material Facts and Issues Presented

 

Petitioner, dba COMPANY A, is a sole proprietor who operates a limited-service restaurant and bar that sells wine and beer, in CITY, Texas. In August 2017, Staff initiated a sales and use tax compliance audit of Petitioner for the period of November 1, 2014, through March 31, 2020. Petitioner provided bank statements for a portion of the audit period, 1040 Schedule C’s for 2014-2018, point-of-sale (POS) reports for February 2017-May 2018, food purchase invoices for August 2018-December 2018; and COMPANY B purchase history for 2018 and 2019.

 

Because Petitioner failed to provide complete records, the auditor estimated his sales and purchases using industry averages for costs of goods sold for food, five months of food purchase invoices, and Petitioner’s vendor-reported purchases of alcoholic beverages and tobacco as reflected in the Comptroller’s Retail Inventory Tracking System (RITS).

 

When reviewing the aforementioned documentation, the auditor determined that taxable sales were grossly underreported. RITS data and sales prices were used to calculate monthly beer and wine sales, and a 5% allowance was applied for undocumented losses. The food purchase invoices Petitioner provided were averaged to calculate average monthly food purchases. The industry average for food costs was approximately 35% of food sales. Average monthly food purchases were divided by 35% to arrive at monthly average food sales. Total alcohol sales, minus the 5% allowance, were added to estimated food sales to calculate total taxable sales, and then reported sales were subtracted from total taxable sales to calculate additional taxable sales per month. The applicable tax rate was applied to calculate additional tax due and adjustments were scheduled in Exam 100.

 

During the audit, Petitioner told the auditor that he gave away alcoholic drinks during the audit period but did not document complimentary servings. Petitioner also informed the auditor that due to a fire in January of 2017, he did not open his restaurant to customers during January and February of 2017. The auditor found that no food purchases were made during these two months and purchases made during those two months for alcohol were provided at no cost to workers that assisted in the rebuild of the restaurant. Accordingly, the auditor scheduled adjustments for those alcohol purchases as Taxable Purchase – Comp Drinks in Exam 200.

 

On December 11, 2020, Staff issued an audit report and Texas Notification of Audit Results to Petitioner, assessing sales and use tax, 10% penalty, 50% penalty, and accrued interest. There were 35 delinquent returns or late payments during the audit period, which exceeded 33% of all filing periods. Additionally, the error rate for the audit was 64.25% and the 50% fraud penalty was assessed because Staff determined that Petitioner grossly underreported taxable sales.

 

Petitioner timely requested redetermination and provided some sales reports and bank statements. The audit was amended, and Exams 100 and 200 were removed and replaced with Exam 300, Additional Taxable Sales – Bank Reconciliation, and Exam 400 Additional Taxable Sales – Estimate). The amended audit resulted in a reduction of the tax liability and a reduction of the error rate to 44.22%.

 

Petitioner contends that the amended audit contains error for the following reasons: the auditor only assessed tax on single beers, while it also sells beer in 6-packs, 12-packs, cases, and kegs; it did not account for sales of raw meats and packs of meat; and it erroneously assessed tax on single products that should be assessed as one final product. During conversations with Staff, Petitioner also requested waiver of penalties and interest. Staff did not agree to any further adjustments and referred the matter to SOAH.

 

D. Analysis and Recommendation

 

The law requires retailers to keep records that reflect the total gross receipts from sales and the total purchases of taxable items. Tex. Tax Code § 151.025; 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.281(b). If a taxpayer fails to keep accurate records of gross receipts, gross purchases, deductions, and exclusions, the Comptroller may take actions that include estimating the person’s tax liability based on any available information. Tex. Tax Code §§ 111.0042(d), .008(a). Staff’s evidence is sufficient to establish, prima facie, that the estimated audit was authorized by statute and that the best information available was utilized. Therefore, it is Petitioner’s burden to show audit error by a preponderance of the evidence. 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 1.26(e).

 

Here, Petitioner contends that error exists in the amended audit for the reasons listed above. However, he failed to provide any evidence in the written submission hearing to support its contentions or to otherwise demonstrate audit error. The ALJ recommends affirming the amended assessment.

 

Late penalties are automatically imposed on delinquent taxes. Tex. Tax Code § 111.061. The Comptroller has the discretionary authority to waive late penalties if a taxpayer has exercised reasonable diligence to comply with tax laws. Id. § 111.103. A taxpayer must establish reasonable diligence by a preponderance of evidence. See Comptroller’s Decision No. 117,630 (2024).

 

In making the reasonable diligence determination, the Comptroller considers the following factors: the taxpayer’s audit history, the tax issues involved, whether a change in Comptroller policy occurred during the audit period, whether changes in the law took effect during the audit period, the size and sophistication of the taxpayer, whether tax was collected but not remitted, whether returns were timely filed, the completeness of the taxpayer’s records, the taxpayer’s efforts to comply with the recordkeeping requirements of this state, delinquencies in other taxes, reliance on advice provided by the Comptroller’s office, and the error rate in the current audit. 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.5(b)(3), (d).

 

In the instant audit, Petitioner had 35 delinquent returns or late filings, which exceeded 33% of all periods in the audit, and the amended audit resulted in an overall error rate of 44.22%. Submitting a late return reflects a fundamental lack of reasonable diligence. See, e.g., Comptroller’s Decision No. 113,844 (2018). The ALJ finds that penalty waiver is not warranted in the instant matter because Petitioner failed to establish that it exercised reasonable diligence to comply with tax laws.

 

Delinquent taxes draw interest beginning 60 days after the date due. Tex. Tax Code § 111.060(c). The Comptroller has discretionary authority to waive interest assessments and may exercise his discretion if interest was imposed as a result of undue delay caused by Comptroller personnel, reliance on advice provided by the Comptroller’s office, or natural disaster. Id. § 111.103; 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.5(e). To prevail, Petitioner must demonstrate, by a preponderance of the evidence, that interest waiver is warranted. See 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 1.26(e); see also Comptroller’s Decision Nos. 102,268 and 109,069 (2014). Petitioner identified no factors or legal grounds for a waiver of interest, and the ALJ recommends interest waiver be denied, except as agreed to by Staff.

 

Texas Tax Code § 111.061(b) authorizes a 50% penalty when the failure to pay the tax or file a report when due is the result of fraud or intent to evade the tax. Staff bears the burden to prove by clear and convincing evidence that the imposition of additional penalty for willful or fraudulent failure to pay tax is warranted. 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 1.26(b)(1). Clear and convincing evidence is evidence demonstrating that the thing to be proved is highly probable. See Black’s Law Dictionary (12th ed. 2024); see also Comptroller’s Decision No. 101,773 (2011).

 

The Comptroller has repeatedly held that gross underreporting, along with other factors or no plausible explanation, sufficiently indicates intent to evade the tax and warrants the assessment of the fraud penalty. See, e.g., Comptroller’s Decision No. 111,804 (2015). The Tax Code defines gross error to mean that, after correction of the error, the amount due and payable exceeds the amount initially reported by at least 25%. Tex. Tax Code § 111.205(b). The term “plausible” is defined as conceivably true or successful; possibly correct or even likely; reasonable; and reasonably convincing and seemingly truthful, though possibly mendacious. See Black’s Law Dictionary (12th ed. 2024).

 

In this case, Petitioner’s overall error rate was over 44%, and Petitioner provided no explanation, plausible or otherwise, for the gross underreporting. The ALJ finds the underreporting at issue in this case was the result of fraud or intent to evade the tax.

 

III. Findings of Fact


	
	************** (Petitioner) dba COMPANY A, is a sole proprietor who operates a limited-service restaurant and bar that sells wine and beer, in CITY, Texas.
	
	
	In August 2017, the Tax Division (Staff) of the Texas Comptroller of Public Accounts (Comptroller) initiated a sales and use tax compliance audit of Petitioner for the period of November 1, 2014, through March 31, 2020.
	
	
	Petitioner provided bank statements for a portion of the audit period, 1040 Schedule C’s for 2014-2018, point-of-sale (POS) reports for February 2017-May 2018, food purchase invoices for August 2018-December 2018; and COMPANY B purchase history for 2018 and 2019.
	
	
	Because Petitioner’s documentation was incomplete, the auditor estimated Petitioner’s sales and purchases using industry averages for costs of goods sold for food, five months of food purchase invoices, and Petitioner’s vendor-reported purchases of alcoholic beverages and tobacco as reflected in the Comptroller’s Retail Inventory Tracking System (RITS).
	
	
	The auditor determined that taxable sales were grossly underreported.
	
	
	RITS data and sales prices were used to calculate monthly beer and wine sales, and a 5% allowance was applied for undocumented losses. The food purchase invoices Petitioner provided were averaged to calculate average monthly food purchases. The industry average for food costs was approximately 35% of food sales. Average monthly food purchases were divided by 35% to arrive at monthly average food sales. Total alcohol sales, minus the 5% allowance, were added to estimated food sales to calculate total taxable sales, and then reported sales were subtracted from total taxable sales to calculate additional taxable sales per month. The applicable tax rate was applied to calculate additional tax due and adjustments were scheduled in Exam 100.
	
	
	Petitioner told the auditor that he gave complimentary drinks but did not document any complimentary servings. Petitioner also informed the auditor that due to a fire in January of 2017, it did not open its restaurant to customers during January and February of 2017. The auditor found that no food purchases were made during these two months and purchases made during those two months for alcohol were provided at no cost to workers that assisted in the rebuild of the restaurant. Accordingly, the auditor scheduled adjustments for those alcohol purchases as Taxable Purchase – Comp Drinks in Exam 200.
	
	
	December 11, 2020, Staff issued an audit report and Texas Notification of Audit Results to Petitioner, assessing sales and use tax, 10% penalty, 50% penalty, and accrued interest.
	
	
	There were 35 delinquent returns or late payments during the audit period, which exceeded 33% of all filing periods. The error rate for the audit was 64.25% and the 50% fraud penalty was assessed because Staff determined that Petitioner grossly underreported taxable sales.
	
	
	Petitioner timely requested redetermination and provided some sales reports and bank statements.
	
	
	The audit was amended, and Exams 100 and 200 were removed and replaced with Exam 300, Additional Taxable Sales – Bank Reconciliation, and Exam 400 Additional Taxable Sales – Estimate). The amended audit resulted in a reduction of the tax liability and a reduction of the error rate to 44.22%.
	
	
	Staff did not agree to adjust the amended assessment and referred the matter to the State Office of Administrative Hearings (SOAH).
	
	
	On August 26, 2025, Staff issued a Notice of Hearing by Written Submission. The notice contained a statement of the nature of the hearing; a statement of the legal authority and jurisdiction under which the hearing was to be held; a reference to the particular sections of the statutes and rules involved; and a short, plain statement of the factual matters asserted, or an attachment that incorporated by reference the factual matters asserted in the complaint or petition filed with the state agency.
	
	
	On September 27, 2025, Administrative Law Judge (ALJ) Kimberly LaFlair issued an order setting the written submission hearing.
	
	
	Petitioner did not submit an exhibit list, evidentiary exhibits, or argument for the written submission hearing.
	
	
	The contested case record closed on November 17, 2025.
	


 

IV. Conclusions of Law


	
	The Comptroller has jurisdiction over this matter. See Tex. Tax Code ch. 111.
	
	
	SOAH has jurisdiction over matters related to the hearing in this matter, including the authority to issue a proposal for decision with findings of fact and conclusions of law. See Tex. Gov’t Code ch. 2003.
	
	
	Staff provided proper and timely notice of the hearing. See Tex. Gov’t Code ch. 2001; Tex. Tax Code § 111.009.
	
	
	The law requires retailers to keep records that reflect the total gross receipts from sales and the total purchases of taxable items. Tex. Tax Code § 151.025; 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.281(b).
	
	
	If a taxpayer fails to keep accurate records of gross receipts, gross purchases, deductions, and exclusions, the Comptroller may take actions that include estimating the person’s tax liability based on any available information. Tex. Tax Code §§ 111.0042(d), .008(a).
	
	
	Staff’s evidence is sufficient to establish, prima facie, that the estimated audit was authorized by statute and that the best information available was utilized.
	
	
	It is Petitioner’s burden to show audit error by a preponderance of the evidence. 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 1.26(e).
	
	
	Petitioner failed to provide any evidence in the written submission hearing, and the ALJ recommends affirming the amended assessment.
	
	
	Late penalties are automatically imposed on delinquent taxes. Tex. Tax Code § 111.061. The Comptroller has the discretionary authority to waive late penalties if a taxpayer has exercised reasonable diligence to comply with tax laws. Tex. Tax Code § 111.103. A taxpayer must establish reasonable diligence by a preponderance of evidence. See Comptroller’s Decision No. 117,630 (2024).
	
	
	In making the reasonable diligence determination, the Comptroller considers the following factors: the taxpayer’s audit history, the tax issues involved, whether a change in Comptroller policy occurred during the audit period, whether changes in the law took effect during the audit period, the size and sophistication of the taxpayer, whether tax was collected but not remitted, whether returns were timely filed, the completeness of the taxpayer’s records, the taxpayer’s efforts to comply with the recordkeeping requirements of this state, delinquencies in other taxes, reliance on advice provided by the Comptroller’s office, and the error rate in the current audit. 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.5(b)(3), (d).
	
	
	The ALJ finds that penalty waiver is not warranted in the instant matter because Petitioner failed to establish that it exercised reasonable diligence to comply with tax laws.
	
	
	Delinquent taxes draw interest beginning 60 days after the date due. Tex. Tax Code § 111.060(c).
	
	
	The Comptroller has discretionary authority to waive interest assessments and may exercise his discretion if interest was imposed as a result of undue delay caused by Comptroller personnel, reliance on advice provided by the Comptroller’s office, or natural disaster. Tex. Tax Code § 111.103; 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.5(e).
	
	
	To prevail, Petitioner must demonstrate, by a preponderance of the evidence, that interest waiver is warranted. See 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 1.26(e); see also Comptroller’s Decision Nos. 102,268 and 109,069 (2014).
	
	
	Petitioner identified no factors or legal grounds for a waiver of interest, and the ALJ recommends interest waiver be denied, except as agreed to by Staff.
	
	
	Texas Tax Code § 111.061(b) authorizes a 50% penalty when the failure to pay the tax or file a report when due is the result of fraud or intent to evade the tax. Staff bears the burden to prove by clear and convincing evidence that the imposition of additional penalty for willful or fraudulent failure to pay tax is warranted. 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 1.26(b)(1).
	



	
	Clear and convincing evidence is evidence demonstrating that the thing to be proved is highly probable. See Black’s Law Dictionary (12th ed. 2024); see also Comptroller’s Decision No. 101,773 (2011).
	



	
	The Comptroller has repeatedly held that gross underreporting, along with other factors or no plausible explanation, sufficiently indicates intent to evade the tax and warrants the assessment of the fraud penalty. See, e.g., Comptroller’s Decision No. 111,804 (2015).
	



	
	The Tax Code defines gross error to mean that, after correction of the error, the amount due and payable exceeds the amount initially reported by at least 25%. Tex. Tax Code § 111.205(b).
	



	
	The term “plausible” is defined as conceivably true or successful; possibly correct or even likely; reasonable; and reasonably convincing and seemingly truthful, though possibly mendacious. See Black’s Law Dictionary (12th ed. 2024).
	



	
	Petitioner provided no explanation, plausible or otherwise, for the gross underreporting, and the ALJ finds the underreporting at issue in this case was the result of fraud or intent to evade the tax.
	


 

Signed November 21, 2025.

 

Kimberly LaFlair 

Presiding Administrative Law Judge

 

 



 

 


ENDNOTES



[1] The date calculated is 25 days after this decision is signed. See APA, Tex. Gov’t Code § 2001.146(a); S.B. 1095, Acts 2017, 85th Leg.   For additional guidance, refer to the Frequently Asked Questions Related to Motions for Rehearing, found here: http://comptroller.texas.gov/taxes/publications/96-1789.pdf



[2] At present, insufficient information is available to determine which items and amounts are disputed or undisputed for purposes of Tex. Tax Code, Ch. 112. In the absence of this information, the Comptroller will assume the entire amount of the assessment, as it appears in Comptroller’s Decision Attachment A, the Notification of Hearing Results, remains in dispute.

If Petitioner intends to sue the comptroller to dispute an amount of tax, penalty, or interest assessed in a deficiency redetermination or jeopardy determination under Tex. Tax Code, Ch. 111, Petitioner is required to file a motion for rehearing that “states the specific grounds of error and the disputed amounts associated with the grounds of error.” Tex. Tax Code § 112.201(a)(3). Petitioner should refer to Tex. Tax Code, Ch. 112, for further guidance regarding a suit after redetermination.



[3] See Tex. Tax Code § 111.0081(c).
